claim: Au contacts show up to 15 percent conductance reduction at 20 T
what_is_supported: The phenomenon exists statistically in the reported dataset
what_is_missing: Raw histogram data and uncertainty on the effect size are not available here

claim: Pure Au and Ag cannot explain the observed shift
what_is_supported: The paper reports weak field dependence for clean-junction calculations
what_is_missing: Independent reproduction of those calculations is absent here
competing_explanation: Alternative clean-junction geometries not sampled in the paper

claim: O2 near the contact can produce lower conductance
what_is_supported: A modeled O2 geometry gives about 0.8 G0 and spin asymmetry
what_is_missing: How often such geometries form in the experiment is not shown
competing_explanation: Another adsorbate class could do the same

claim: O2 is the main magnetic adsorbate candidate
what_is_supported: O2 is the obvious magnetic residual-gas species among common contaminants
what_is_missing: Actual chamber composition at the junction is not measured

claim: Field biases O2 configurations enough to shift the histogram
what_is_supported: This is the bridge needed to explain the experiment
what_is_missing: No direct occupancy model or adsorption statistics are supplied

claim: Orientation anisotropy of O2 helps select the low-conductance state
what_is_supported: The paper reports anisotropy energies around 10^-4 eV for favorable geometries
what_is_missing: Whether that energy scale is sufficient at 4.2 K is not quantified
competing_explanation: The anisotropy may be too small to dominate occupancy

claim: Observed conductance reduction comes from state selection more than direct field tuning
what_is_supported: This best matches the paper's own narrative
what_is_missing: The paper does not present an explicit state-population model
competing_explanation: Direct magnetotransport of a fixed decorated junction
